narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and an LSAT score of 29, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your plans to attend full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 42% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger a rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your background that stood out most strongly to the model. The biggest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank of around 5.5, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustaining strong academic performance over time. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your family income in the low bracket, which actually nudged the prediction toward passing, as the model has seen students from similar backgrounds succeed when other factors align. Your undergraduate GPA also worked in your favor, pulling the odds slightly toward bar passage since it's close to the average of 3.21, showing you have a foundation of capability. Compared to typical students who get approved, your lower Year 3 decile is what sets you apart most dramatically, as stronger late-stage grades are a hallmark of those predicted to pass the bar easily. While your LSAT is below the average of 36.7 and your Year 1 decile of 2 trails the norm of 5.7, these played smaller roles here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or lawyer—it's the model weighing patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score or strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, which could reshape your profile for future applications. Many applicants in your shoes have succeeded by targeting these areas, and law schools value persistence. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that look beyond predictive models. Keep pushing; your full-time commitment is a great sign you're ready to invest fully.
